entry.title: Restart my Leetcode journey
entry.summary:
Restart my Leetcode journey:

It has been over 2 years of my last leetcode submission.I wasn't aware I will come back. After going back to be an engineer, I found how weak I am and how useful it is of the algorithms and Data Structures. Going back to Leetcode …
entry.content:
Restart my Leetcode journey:

It has been over 2 years of my last leetcode submission.I wasn't aware I will come back. After going back to be an engineer, I found how weak I am and how useful it is of the algorithms and Data Structures. Going back to Leetcode is difficult. It looks like you know everything, but you just cannot remember when you should brute force to solve it asap.(I mean from a context perspective)

I would always suggest to have a book instead of randomly find some resources from Internet. At least, the sequence of the topic has been organized. It comes with a purpose, maybe the level of difficulty.

entry.title: Event-Driven architecture
entry.content:
Event-Driven architecture

To start an event-driven architecture application. We have to understand few things. 1. what is a event 2. how do we receive an event 3. how do we process the event after computation 'completed'.

entry.title: First Post on My learning New Blog
entry.content:
I am On My Way To logging my larning note

This is the initial post for the Blog. In a short period, the following topic will be my main focus of my study(all in python).

  1. event-driven architecture
  2. domain-driven architecture
  3. functional programming
  4. design pattern revisit

entry.title: Long execution time
entry.summary:
How to dealing with a long execution time sql

First thing, identify locking and waiting object.We have to make sure if the sql will lock or wait any table.

The easiest way is to extract the explained plan.Normally, you will only get the estimated plan. In Postgresql, you …
entry.content:
How to dealing with a long execution time sql

First thing, identify locking and waiting object.We have to make sure if the sql will lock or wait any table.

The easiest way is to extract the explained plan.Normally, you will only get the estimated plan. In Postgresql, you can use "analyze" to execute your sql once and get a real plan with execution time. *Diff SQL will have diff behaiver, but basically it will show you the sequence and the cost of every step

we can find from the documenation: "ANALYZE requires only a read lock on the target table, so it can run in parallel with other activity on the table." which means we can mimic the process without a huge impact to the system.

Second, a bad index and bad constraint can impact the execution time. Fragmenation is always the first thing of a bad index, we should always aware of that and rebuild the index regularly)especially in a operational DB.
